text:p.text:span: saying (the words of) Åarrum-bani, ­royal commissioner, your servant:
You commissioned me to carry out construction on the great­ fortifications of Muriq-Tidnim
and presented your views to me as follows: “The Amorites have repeatedly raided the frontier territory.”­
to rebuild the fortifications, to cut off their
and thus to prevent them from repeatedly overwhelming the fields through­ a breach (in the defenses) between theTigris and Euphrates.
As I was leaving (for the assignment),
from the banks of the Abgal canal up thought to the ­territory of Zimudar,
When I had been working on the fortifications that then measured 26
after having reached (the area) between the two mountain ranges,­
the Amorite camped in the mountains turned his attention to my building activities.
(The leader of) Simurum came to his aid, and ­
he went out against me between the mountain ranges of Ebih­ to do battle.
And therefore I, even thought I could not
went out to confront him in ­battle.
so that I can get to work
Although I have not been able to reach
of the frontier territory, [as soon as I received] information,
But the territory has changed its allegiance,
and so I have not neglected to build the­ fortifications— (to the contrary), I have been building and fighting (at the same time).
Even if (another) royal commissioner were to be selected from the grandest of (military) units
he would still not be able to attain the goal of preventing the frontier territory from changing its allegiance
After I dispatched my envoy to you,
right behind him I dispatched (another) envoy to Lu-Nanna, the governor of Zimudar, and
he sent me a very large contingent (viz. 7200) of workers.
There are enough corvée laborers but not enough fighting men!
Once my king gives the orders to­ release the workers (for military duty),
then when (the enemy) raids, I shall be able to fight him!
) dispatched the (same) man to the nobles of your frontier territory ­
and they presented their case to me as follows:
“We cannot even guard all the cities by ourselves,
They then sent my messenger back (to me) though him.
Ever since my king commanded me,
day and night I have been diligently doing the assigned work as well as fighting (the enemy).
Because I am obedient to my king’s command (to build the fortifications) and I continue to
even though the requisite force has not been assigned to me, I will not cease fighting
Now my king is informed (about all of this)!
Var: May the storm (of my king) cover the land!
At the present time this letter is only attested in sources from Ur and from tablets of uncertain provenience but it was also known to the compiler of the
[…] (followed by the incipit of the response).
is used in such contexts to introduce direct speech, as in PuIb1 (23), although the sources differ on the person who is speaking. In X1 it is the messenger (
), in X3 it is the king
), and Ur1 has a hybrid form (
). On the other hand the parallel use of
(line 7) in ArÅ1: 8 (1) suggests that we are dealing with indirect speech. The variants in all manuscripts compound the problem.
is used here in the sense of “send work orders;” X1 must have had
, “sent me an order.” The subordinate temporal clauses in line 5 require a main clause, and if this is
, then only the first sentence is a direct quote and the rest is a summary of the instructions.
. The reconstructed text follows Ur1. The scribe of X2 either­ missed two signs or understood the text differently: “so that­ from a breach in the Tigris and Euphrates waters
should not­ overwhelm (the land/fields).” Note that
is far from certain, as the second sign looks more like
. The verb in both lines must be
, “to rise (transitive and intransitive), levy, etc.” For the form in line 8 compare
, “when you arise like the south wind.” The meaning here probably reflects the Akkadian use of
, in the meaning “to set out, depart, leave” (
T 311). In line 10 it reflects the more common Sumerian usage, meaning “to levy,” (

). This conforms to what Ur III sources tell us about Åarrum-bani. Presumably he was stationed at Apiak, on the Abgal, when he received his order from the king and he apparently levied workers as he made his way to Zimudar.
. In Ur III, as in later times, the
was ­equivalent to ca. 10  km.; see, most recently, R. Englund,
31 (1988) 168 n. 40. Therefore, according to this text, the fortifications­, at this point, measured ca. 269 km. in length. Note the writing
, which is standard in OB but in Ur III is used only at Nippur; it is regularly written
) in all other archives of that time. On these lines see P. Attinger,
) but it is more likely that this is an example of a morpho-phonemic writing.
. The reading of the first signs of the final verb is uncertain. I have read
. One could translate: “and as for me, as soon as I had released … the corvée workers.”
, “corvée,” see the comments on PuÅ1: 27 (13) above. Such workers, like
, could do both labor and military duties.
. The second verb in the line is difficult. Normally,
is interpreted as “to win, oppress, defeat” (F. Karahashi,
76–77), and this would result in a translation “he will reinforce my laborers
so that he will assure my victory
.” While I would not rule this out, with some reservations I would suggest that
Å /1 136). Although at present this meaning for the Akkadian is attested only in NA, see already
…] (OBGT XI iii 12). Perhaps this means simply, “to provide wages,” and the line should be translated as: “he will reinforce my laborers and provide me with the (appropriate) wages.”
has been discussed numerous times,­ without a final consensus as to its meaning; see, most recently­, J. Klein,

126. It appears that two different words have ­to be posited, an adjective that has been translated as­ “valiant,” “mighty,” or as a noun. The latter has been ­rendered as “tower” by Klein and others but this is a guess only.
, “to alter the allegiance/state of the frontier/territory,” which is not attested in Sumerian outside of CKU,
; on the Akkadian expression, which is limited to omen texts, see J. Bottéro, in A. Finet, ed.,

office:text.text:p: 24. In the lexical tradition the word im-ri-a is generally rendered by kimtu, šalãtu, and ništu; see the dictionaries and Å. Sjöberg, HSAO 1 202–9. Prior to Ur III it is written as im-ru-a; this is still the case in Gudea and in the common Ur III PN lugal-im-ru-a, at Girsu, Drehem, Ur, Nippur, and in the Turam-ili archive. In Ur III im-ri-a is attested only in single occurrence of the PN lugal-im-ri-a (AUCT 1 300: 5) from Drehem, and twice in a summary of an Umma legal proceeding, NSGU 201 (TCL 5 6059). While generally translated as “family, kin-group,” or the like, the exact meaning of im-ri-a in early texts is difficult to determine. The Gudea Cylinders include mention of the im-ri-a of Ningirsu, Nanše, and Inana (Cyl. A xvi 18–27); these seem to be corporate groups, not necessarily related by kinship ties, that have labor and possibly military obligations to the state. Something similar seems to be the case in Åulgi Hymn B 98–99: Ñiåkak-pana-ta zú-kéå-Ñá na-me im-ri-a diå-àm lú na-ma-ta-éd-e, “And as for (shooting) arrows, not one man from any unit among my regiments could outdo me!” Here there can be no doubt that im-ri-a is a military unit of some kind; it might be kin based but that is something that cannot be established at present.

text:p.text:span: In this line the term is qualified by the adjective

, which is usually found in geographical names and in cultic texts but is rare in main dialect Sumerian school compositions. The meaning of the word is still open to debate; it is usually viewed as a phonetic variant of
but it is more likely that it is a superlative, as in
, is one of the various words for “lion” in Sumerian (see my forthcoming study of this feline terminology). Note, in epistolary literature,
nu-zu nam-tag-Ñá Ñéåtu la-ba-åi-Ñál, nam-im-ri-a åár-ré

, which I would translate, with all due caution, as “I have no idea of my transgression, I have not been told of (the true nature of my) transgression; because (of the shame that this has brought) on (my) numerous family, I am consumed with sorrow.” It is therefore possible that the present line also refers to a kin group rather than a military unit.
. The verb in line 25 and the syntax of both lines are problematical. The form
is third person, and although the non-perfect form normally has the agent
) is used with a prefixed agent with the non-perfect root in OB Sumerian literary texts.
is used here in the sense of Akkadian
, “to sufficient for,” a usage that is probably derived from Akkadian—almost always in the form
—and found mainly in logographic usage in mathematical school texts; e.g.,
as a verbal rather than adjectival root is likewise an Akkadism; it is based on the use of
(normally rendered in lexical and bilingual texts by
). Compare, for example, the Old Babylonian letter
apir have assigned me 16 workers, I still have five
too little.” Indeed, the phraseology of this line brings to mind the logographic
, “let it be more or less,” in Old Babylonian documents (
; it is used when releasing people from work or obligations. See
, “when school was let out, I would go home.”

. The shifters seem confusing but it would appear that Åarrum-bani sent a messenger to Lu-Nanna asking for troops. The latter, in turn, addressed the nobles of his land, who replied that they could not spare any soldiers, addressing Åarrum-bani as well as Lu-Nanna. It may be that the Lu-Nanna and Åarrum-bani letter (LuÅa1, 20) contains that response. The

of line 33 must be the messenger as the one in line 37; it would appear that after hearing out Åarrum-bani’s envoy, he sent him on to the nobles, and they, in turn, had him return directly to Åarrum-bani.
. Alternative translation: “Once my king (issues me new) orders,
night and day ­I will do the assigned work and fight (the enemy).”
; see the comments of E. Sollberger.
. I know of only one other occurrence of
, “when he is armed with his righteous staff and with his beloved weapon
The finite verbal form (in the meaning “to cease”) occurs almost exclusively with the negative prefix; hence the latter variant is­ preferred. For examples, see
64. It is possible that the author of X3 interpreted the verb as
, “to be mighty.” The translation of this line is uncertain; alternatively, one could also propose a rendition “
even though I have inadequate forces at my disposal, I will not be finished off by force.
The tablet has deteriorated since it was copied.

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablets (Type I): X1, X3, rest Type III (X2 landscape).

text:p.text:span: X1 . ni . . + + . o o o . . . o o o

office:text.text:p: X1 o o . . o o o o o
Ur1 + + + + + + + . o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: Ur1 + + + + + x o o o o i + + - + . o o o

office:text.text:p: Ur1 + + + + + + + . o o o o o

Ur1 + . . + + . o o o o o o o .

Ur1 . . o o o o o o o . o o o o
Ur1 + + . . o o o o o o o o o o
Ur1 + + . . . . o o o o o o

text:p.text:span: A piece of clay has been glued in the break, possibly in the place of
It seems that the scribe had problems with this sign and wrote
